headline: California Condor Soars Back From Brink of Extinction
meta_description: The California condor population has grown from just 22 birds in 1982 to 607 today, with a wild breeding pair now nesting in the Pacific Northwest.
summary_short: Once down to 22 birds, California condors are recovering, with a wild pair now incubating the first wild egg in the Pacific Northwest in over a century.
body:
In 1982, the California condor stood on the precipice of extinction with only 22 birds remaining. Decades of effort to breed them in captivity and carefully reintroduce them to the wild have yielded extraordinary progress. Today, the population has grown to 607 birds, a remarkable turnaround for one of the world's rarest and largest flying birds.

A particularly hopeful sign comes from the Pacific Northwest, where the Yurok Tribe has been involved in reintroduction efforts. A pair of condors has now laid and begun incubating an egg in the wild—the first wild-laid egg in that region in more than a century. This milestone suggests that the birds are not merely surviving in captivity, but beginning to establish themselves again in their natural habitat.

The condor's recovery demonstrates what sustained commitment to wildlife conservation can achieve. From the brink of permanent loss, these magnificent birds are reclaiming their place in wild skies.
